title: Hierarchical Modeling

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Hierarchical (mixed-effects) models account for the nested structure of IVT experiments: reactions within wells, wells within plates, plates within sessions. This correctly handles correlations and variance at each level.

sections.heading: Variance Components
sections.content: The model separates total variance into components:

subsections.heading: Session Variance
subsections.content: Day-to-day variation in reagent preparation, instrument calibration, and lab conditions.

subsections.heading: Plate Variance
subsections.content: Plate-to-plate variation within a session, including edge effects and handling differences.

subsections.heading: Residual Variance
subsections.content: Well-to-well variation not explained by session or plate effects.

sections.heading: MCMC Diagnostics
sections.content: Bayesian estimation uses MCMC sampling. Check R-hat < 1.01 (convergence), ESS > 400 (sufficient samples), and zero divergent transitions (sampling stability).

sections.heading: Interpreting Results
sections.content: Fold changes are reported with 95% credible intervals. P(FC > 0) indicates directional certainty. P(|FC| > threshold) indicates probability of meaningful effect.

why_it_matters: Hierarchical modeling provides honest uncertainty estimates by accounting for all sources of variation. Ignoring hierarchy leads to overconfident (too narrow) confidence intervals.
